replies.author: @Siracusano
replies.text:
Short answer: Hollywood already stole it.

The core idea a “good” respectable man with a hidden, violent alter ego,  basically became its own genre (Fight Club, the Hulk, Venom, Black Swan, every prestige show about a double life). So doing a straight Jekyll & Hyde now risks feeling like a cliché of its own influence.

Also, the original horror is psychological, not flashy, it’s mostly guilt, addiction, and identity collapse, not castles, lightning labs, or vampires, which makes it harder to sell as spectacle. The famous twist (Jekyll = Hyde) is no longer a twist because everyone already knows it. It’s uncomfortable to market because the monster and the victim are the same person so there’s no clean hero. And the story ends in total self-destruction which is terrible for sequels.

So it’s not that it “doesn’t work.” It worked so well it got absorbed into everything else. Jekyll & Hyde basically dissolved into the entire modern antihero.

replies.author: @RinkyJohann-u6t
replies.text: The creature doesn't seek revenge. Since his birth he is lonely. And because every human, even Victor his nominal father, that he encounters fears and rejects him, he wants a mate of his own kind. Only Victor knows this science of reanimation but he refuses to do the creature's bidding, fearing a whole breed of monsters coming out of the unholy union. For that, the monster spends the rest of the novel killing everyone Victor has ever loved. It is Victor who seeks revenge on the creature, hence their cat-and-mouse chase across the North Pole.

replies.text:
​@blueconversechucks I just saw Frankenstein and while it is very professionally made movie (visuals, acting, sound, music etc. etc.) it made no sense in most scenes (in logic sense).

Victor trying to kill a monster by shooting at it? Which he had previous tried to explode and which had just told Victor that it cannot be killed in any basic means because it had already tried to die?
Er, why...? First Victor tried killing it because it was too stupid for his taste, when it proved it was not stupid he still continued murdering because... Elizabeth seemed to fell in love the dead thing at first sight or something?

Where did the superhero like magical regenerative abilities to the monster come from? As we know death flesh is great of creating new matter out of thin air?

Pan's labyrinth is great. It is inherently magical tale. Toro makes visually (and conceptually) great stuff, while many of his creations make zero sense in the setting he had created. Including e.g. Pacific Rim which I would have loved to love as an idea (mecha's v. kaiju's – sign me right in), but it lacked of ANY coherent internal logic so it hurt to actually watch it especially after the mecha pulled the kaiju deleting blade out of it's ass when it was plot convenient to do. Causes constant brain damage to even remember that shait.

replies.author: @GideonRavenor712
replies.text:
I enjoyed the Golden Army myself, I have a soft spot for celtic dark fantasy inspirations, and the makeup and practical work was fantastic, though I do understand why people think it's the lesser of the Del Toro Hellboys (Still LEAGUES better than the latest one Del Toro didn't do, but yea).

-Ends on a set of pretty big cliff hangers we're never gonna see resolved in film
-The short-lived sub-plot to try and make Hellboy and co. more sympathetic towards Nuada's spite towards humans felt a bit forced
-Several of the secondary characters feel like they got their backstories or subplots condensed for time (Krauss for example, he's interesting, but we kinda needed a bit more detail as to why he changes his mind besides a pair of rings and one set of lines of him losing a loved one. It works, but it feels cut.)

And then there's the plot hole that if they'd just handed Naula's piece of the crown to Liz for her to melt during ANY of the time between the Troll Market and the fight with Nauda in Section 51, Nauda's plan would be screwed and the Golden Army useless. I can't even think of a reason why Naula wouldn't have done this, if the crown pieces CAN be destroyed then destroying even one section renders the whole thing useless. But instead, she tries to hide it before thinking of this.

But all that being said . . . yea, I still enjoy the movie immensely.

replies.text:
@strange-universe The overall conceit of the novel is that it's the report of a journalist who sheds light on peculiar events that happened some time ago in the Parisian opera house. The atmosphere is less "Beware the Phantom of the Operaaaaaaa!" and more "Some strange stuff happened back then, but let me explain rationally how the so-called phantom committed his crimes."

Like The Hunchback of Notre Dame, it can easily be adapted into Gothic horror, but the novel itself is in a different genre.

author: @gristlyknave831
text:
I’m not sure how I feel about this movie. I read the book for the first time at the start of this year and then talk about it last month for a class. Normally seeing the movie after reading the book and seeing the differences doesn’t bother me but for some reason this one does. The original Frankenstein movie is way more different from the book that it’s basically its own thing so it doesn’t bug me as much. But this one has a lot from the book but the changes to them are very different and it just doesn’t sit well with me. 
I did enjoy the first half of the movie more than the second half. The film looked beautiful and I enjoyed the performances.
But what I liked about the story from the book you have the question of who is the monster Frankenstein or his creation; but this movie makes it more clear that it’s Frankenstein that is the monster. I think that’s why I didn’t care for it. I don’t think it’s a bad film by any means and a part of me wonders if I would’ve like it more if I hadn’t read the book first. To me the movie just wasn’t as interesting as the book was.

@mrnobody4125 Strong disagree.  Viktor is no kind of hero, anti- or otherwise.  He's not driven by heroic motivations.  He's never really learns better.  He refuses to cooperate mostly from disgust and then his own rage.  He is arguably most analogous to the worst kind of parent:  neglectful, dismissive, imperceptive, impatient, and ultimately murderous.

While he's not a moustache-twirling villain, part of what so many find captivating about the story is exactly how, although he might seem sympathetic compared to a monster, in the end, he's not.

replies.author: @DeadpoolX9
@mattchandler600
replies.text: This. I read this book for the first time when I was at school (we watched the Branagh film too) and we were all asked to write essays on it. The question we were posed was: 'Who is the real monster? Victor, or the creature?'. People argued one way or the other, but I was the only one in my class who put up my hand and said "well... they BOTH are, in their own way." They both acted monstrously toward one another, and both brought out the absolute worst in each other at different points in the novel. Neither was the hero of the tale, and that was the tragedy of it. I was surprised that nobody else could see that.

replies.author: @ludovico6890
replies.text: ​​@anthonyml7 while Mary Shelley was not atheist herself, neither was she devout. Her husband was an atheist and from what I understand so was her father. There's some religious elements in her famous novels, such as allusion to Adam and his report with God, but overall the whole novel is a proto/crypto science fiction where the menace is entirely naturalistic in nature. Frankenstein is a man of science, for one, not a necromancer. If creating life from death is blasphemy from a religious perspective, that he succeeds to create a man that is physically (if not intellectually) superior to a normal man is also quite telling. It's science and nature that is the superior force man is unable to tame or control, not any spiritual power. And there's also a certain dose of anticlericalism: the only priest mentioned in the novel, from what I remember, is a petty, judgmental, narrow minded confessor who forces an innocent woman to plea guilty to a murder she did not commit. Now compare this to Dracula (a must-read, by the way), where religious symbols are used for effective protection against vampires, where even Protestant England is too secular for its own safety and vulnerable to an ancient evil. Now that is a religious novel, The Exorcist before its time.

text:
It's worth pointing out that Mary Shelley's Frankenstein is itself a reimagining of Paradise Lost by John Milton. The book states this pretty explicitly with a twist: Mary Shelley's monster is like Satan, but he is abandoned by his creator, while Satan rebels against his.

What I really would like to see is a movie adaptation of Paradise Lost. That is a story worth telling.

author: @the_absurd_hero
text:
Just got back from seeing this in theaters:

As a fan of the book, I'm really pleased with how well del Toro adapted it for the screen. Visually, the sets, costumes, props, etc. create a very stylish, gothic world, and I wouldn't be surprised if the film got nominated for costume and/or production design.

The acting was all superb, especially Jacob Elordi's Creature; the physical acting stands out as much as his highly emotive facial expressions. At first, I thought Oscar Issac was a curious choice for the titular role, but his strong performance, replete with subtle transformation from intense genius to cruel "father," erased any qualms I may have had.

Mia Goth presents a wonderful foil as the compassionate, doomed Elizabeth, who could still show very human moments of disdain and fury.

Cons: Some of the CGI doesn't look so hot, but it's thankfully pretty brief. Also, there are some sections of the book that (understandably) were omitted.

Overall, I'd suggest seeing this in theaters if you can.

author: @hawkticus_history_corner

I've always found it interesting that so few versions of this story have ever really tried to just directly adapt the original story. All of them are someone making their own version of the story instead of more or less following what Mary wrote. 

Which I find interesting

replies.author: @ErinUnderwood
replies.text:
The problem is that there are so many story beats in the novel. A film only has room for a certain number of beats and if you make a film that sticks too closely to the source material (especially in a period piece) you run the danger of watching a lot of people sitting around talking for extended periods of time. I think it's because of the vast number of story beats in the novel that make it endlessly fascinating for people to try adapting it to the screen. They can still make it "essentially" faithful to the scope of the novel while still having some creative flexibility. 

Sorry, I did my graduate work on the art of adaptation. I get way to wonky on the topic! LOL

author: @kennethfharkin
text:
I've read the novel and seen several versions, including the Branagh one in the theater.  This is now my favorite telling of this story, including the book.  I have one caveat, for straight up favorite movie this version loses to Mel Brooks' Young Frankenstein but what can you say 🙂

I watched this last night with my wife, who never stays awake and is not a supernatural horror fan.  She stayed up for the whole thing.  Solid acting, story, effects, and cinematography all came together.  The monster starts off as truly that, a monster.  It is terrifying and kills men with ease.  When we get to the monster's story, told from its perspective the monster goes from an it to a he.  We already were gaining some sympathy for it as Victor's tale played out towards the end.  When we see the story pick up with his escape we see something else entirely.  

I especially liked the ending which was Del Toro's twist.  Victor's asking for forgiveness and the creature's granting it called directly back to the old man's tale from the cabin.  Nothing had to be said but at that moment anyone who had watched the film knew exactly what the creature was thinking.  His freeing of the ship and the captain's realization that his obsession with reaching the Pole was a parallel to that of Victor's and it would doom them all gave hope that people can be better and learn.  

Damn, what a fantastic film.

text:
It has the same central characters and basic premise, and SOME of the framing but like every other screen adaptation it is woefully unfaithful to the source material.

The entire backstory, is changed from the source material.  Victor was not driven by his mother's death and he was not was not trying to conquer death.  His goal was scientific achievement and the achievement he was looking for was the creation of human life.  He manufactured the creature and all of its components.  It all went disastrously wrong because Shelley was saying that the creation of human life is the domain of women, not of men, and men can only create a travesty.  This was the feminism of her day.

In English, if you thought someone was exaggerating a point, you might ask: "Why are you making a big deal out of it?"  In French, you would say something like: "Why are you of it making a story?"

replies.author: @MaryRohwer
replies.text: ​@eternalhalloween1Learning the idiom of a language is certainly different than learning it in school.  My father learned German in high school, but when he goes to Germany and speaks, the German people tell him he sounds like he swallowed a textbook.  But... I say he sounds like that in English too, lol.

replies.author: @eternalhalloween1
replies.text: @MaryRohwer Yea. Another good one is that if you were in trouble, you might say: "This is a pretty kettle of fish."  In French what you say is" We're in some handsome curtains."  My older sister taught me her formal  vs familiar hack.  She said: "Simple. If you call someone Mr. or Ms. Mrs.  use formal VOUS.  If you are on a first name basis, use familiar TU.   And yes. Using the formal "you" in the wrong place can be just as rude.  Books often say the "formal" is always safe.  She said: "Trust me. IT ISN'T."

author: @justinklenk
text:
I must say, I expected a farce of a modern reinterpretation of this story. How delightfully - DELIGHTFULLY - wrong I was, despite the big departures from Shelley that I knew were coming. 

This film was nothing less than a WONDERFULLY poignant, further exploration of the real, true essence of the story which Shelley originally laid out for us.

I dare say it is, in my personal opinion, del Toro's _masterpiece_ - as it would seem he himself truly intended. The legitimately conveyed humanity in this retelling falls not below, but rises to MATCH Guillermo's incredibly excellent cinematographic execution, here.

I can't believe I'm saying this about a film in this day and age of _miserably pathetic_ standards across 99.99% of what we still, somehow, call "cinema" - but this was not just a "good"... but I dare say a GREAT film.

_BRAVO,_ Guillermo. SO well done.

author: @NoBuenoMas
text:
Del Toro's Frankenstein was brilliant. The choice to stage the film just a few years further in the future when electricity began to industrialize while marrying that context with Del Toro's signature naturalistic images of life and nature and seeing the contradiction was incredible. When the Creature walks down with the bloodied Elizabeth during the ball, or when gigantic electric conductors encircle him during Frankenstein's experiments, or the wolves / winter snow / violent men symbolize the "miserable life" the Creature laments at ... of this seems to be Del Toro going deeper into truly timeless archetypical analogies that could literally apply to anything of similar context (just imagine the development of artificial intelligence while you watch the film and the symbolism is just amazing to think on). 

It feels truly incredible to see these master filmmakers consider these truly timeless stories with such dignity and reverence (i.e. Nosferatu by Eggers, Pinocchio and Frankenstein by Del Toro).
